If you or someone you know has been involved in an 18-wheeler crash in Austin, Texas, the steps taken in the first 48 hours can make or break your case. Truck accident claims are fundamentally different from standard auto accidents — they involve FMCSA federal regulations, multiple liable parties (driver, carrier, shipper, maintenance company), and critical electronic evidence like black box data that can be overwritten or lost within days.

At WreckMatch, we built a network of 800+ participating law firms specifically to ensure victims are matched with attorneys who have real truck accident litigation experience — not just general personal injury practitioners. Our matching process is free, fast, and carries no upfront cost to the victim.

Key issues Austin truck accident victims need to address immediately:
• Sending a spoliation letter to preserve black box (ECM) data
• Identifying all liable parties under Texas and federal law
• Documenting injuries before insurance adjusters make contact
• Understanding Texas's modified comparative fault rules

Don't navigate this alone. The trucking company's legal team is already working against you. Get matched with an experienced Austin truck accident attorney today.

👉 https://www.wreckmatch.com/#form

reddit_title: Hit by an 18-wheeler in Austin, TX — here's what actually matters in the first 48 hours (2026 breakdown)
reddit_body:
Truck accident cases in Texas are way more complex than regular car crashes and most people don't realize it until it's too late. When an 18-wheeler is involved, you're not just dealing with one driver and one insurance policy — you could have the trucking company, a separate cargo shipper, a maintenance contractor, and multiple insurers all pointing fingers at each other. Under Texas's modified comparative fault rules, they'll try to shift blame onto you if they can.

The single most urgent thing to do is preserve the truck's black box (ECM) data. This records speed, braking, and hours of service right before the crash — but it can be overwritten in as little as 30 days. An attorney needs to send a spoliation letter immediately to legally require the trucking company to preserve it. FMCSA regulations also require carriers to keep driver logs, inspection records, and drug test results — all of which become evidence.

If you're trying to figure out next steps, the site wreckmatch.com has a detailed 2026 victim guide specific to Austin that covers evidence, liable parties, and Texas law. They also match victims with truck accident attorneys at no upfront cost, which is worth knowing if you haven't hired anyone yet. The earlier you move, the better your options.
